does: MIRROR presents a unified red-teaming framework for multimodal agentic RAG systems that combines memory-guided Monte Carlo tree search with a novelty constraint mechanism. The approach uses a Novelty Gate that filters candidate attacks by comparing them against retrieved context under normalized metrics, preventing exact duplication while allowing retrieval to inform search priorities. The method is evaluated across four distinct attack surfaces—text poisoning, image injection, direct-query attacks, and orchestrator-level tool manipulation—achieving 76% attack success rate on image poisoning (versus 52% for baselines), 97% on orchestrator attacks at half the query cost, and maintains the lowest cross-surface performance variance with a coefficient of variation of 0.47, substantially outperforming specialized single-surface baselines.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in red-teaming research where existing methods are surface-specific and heavily recycle known attack templates, exhibiting 73-84% exact duplication on text-poisoning benchmarks. The paper builds on prior Monte Carlo tree search techniques for adversarial generation and retrieval-augmented generation frameworks, extending them to handle the expanded attack surface of multimodal agentic systems. It responds to the evolution of RAG systems from simple retrieval pipelines to complex orchestrators with tool interaction capabilities, and incorporates insights from novelty-seeking search methods to prevent degenerate solutions that plagiarize existing attacks while maintaining the benefits of context-informed generation.
enables: MIRROR establishes a foundation for comprehensive red-teaming of complex agentic systems by demonstrating that unified frameworks can outperform specialized approaches across heterogeneous attack surfaces. The novelty constraint mechanism opens research directions into balancing exploration of genuinely novel vulnerabilities against efficient search in high-dimensional attack spaces. This work enables future development of more sophisticated adversarial discovery methods for RAG systems, generalization to other multimodal AI agents, and potential applications in proactive vulnerability assessment before deployment. The low cross-surface variance suggests the framework could support automated, continuous red-teaming in production systems, and the memory-guided approach provides a template for leveraging historical attack data to improve future discovery efficiency.
